items.title: Affective valence as a computational signal for learning value
items.author: Oriel FeldmanHall
items.content: Reinforcement learning models often ignore affect’s well-established role in shaping behavior. This study shows evidence that affective valence serves as a computational signal that tracks learning in both social and non-social domains alongside reward.

items.title: Individual traits and experiences predict the content of dreams
items.author: Giacomo Handjaras
items.content: Individual traits and experiences jointly shape dream content. By analyzing the semantic structure of thousands of reports, we reveal systematic patterns showing how dreams reflect personality, sleep quality, and external stressors.

items.title: Rebalancing psychology in China
items.author: Xiang-Zhen Kong
items.content: Does the interdisciplinary direction imply the erosion of psychology’s disciplinary foundations, or can the field sustain its identity while expanding its conceptual and methodological boundaries?

items.title: Motivation biases behavior but not perception
items.author: Hugh Riddell
items.content: Across four experiments, this study shows that motivation biases where people look and how they respond to ambiguous stimuli, but not how they perceive them, challenging the notion of motivated perception.

items.title: Genetic and environmental influences on data missingness in developmental cognitive neuroscience
items.author: T. Falck-Ytter
items.content: This twin study investigated sources behind missing data in infant brain and behaviour research. Genetic and shared environmental influences were substantial, highlighting the need for improved procedures to reduce data loss.

items.title: Beliefs about accuracy shape confidence attributions to humans and artificial agents
items.author: Stephen M. Fleming
items.content: This work shows how humans infer others’ confidence from their observed behaviour and prior beliefs about the agents’ accuracy. As a result, they overestimate the confidence of AI agents compared to other humans, even when behaviour is identical—an illusion of AI confidence.

items.title: Type-1 and type-2 decisions feature computational noise of similar magnitude
items.author: Dobromir Rahnev
items.content: Zheng et al. show that confidence (Type-2) judgments feature the same magnitude of computational noise as perceptual (Type-1) judgments, with important implications for the debate on whether Type-1 and Type-2 decisions are made by separate systems. DR: @dobyrahnev.bsky.social KX: @kaixue98.bsky.social

items.title: Perceptual rhythms by phase-aligned perceptual performance peaks across trials
items.author: Stefan Treue
items.content: Brain rhythms may provide temporal windows for the allocation of peaks in perceptual efficiency, even if efficiency itself is not rhythmic. A reanalysis of public datasets shows rhythmicity in temporal priming supporting this view.
